title: SOC 2 CC1.1 — Integrity and ethical values: code-of-conduct acknowledgement trail
exclusions: Does NOT test the substance of the code of conduct; attests that every active employee has acknowledged the current version within policy cadence.
evidence: Saved search 'soc2_cc1_1_coc_ack' (daily) emits per employee: user, hire_date, latest_ack_version, latest_ack_date, is_compliant, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CC1.1 Integrity and ethical values — acknowledgement coverage is the direct evidence.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC-2 CC1.1 (Integrity and ethical values) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.8.36: SOC 2 CC1.1 — Integrity and ethical values: code-of-conduct acknowledgement trail.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.8.36' running on sourcetype hr:employee and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 6.3 Competence — contributing; the CoC is one training module.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 6.3 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.8.36: SOC 2 CC1.1 — Integrity and ethical values: code-of-conduct acknowledgement trail.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.8.36' running on sourcetype hr:employee and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A user with no CoC acknowledgement in 13 months returns is_compliant=0.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A user who acknowledged the current version within the year is absent.

spl:
index=hr sourcetype=hr:employee status=active earliest=-30d
| stats latest(hire_date) AS hire_date BY user
| join user type=outer
  [search index=hr sourcetype=lms:acknowledgement module=code_of_conduct earliest=-2y
   | stats latest(version) AS latest_ack_version latest(_time) AS latest_ack_date BY user]
| lookup coc_versions.csv AS latest OUTPUT current_version
| eval is_compliant=if(latest_ack_version==current_version AND latest_ack_date>now()-31536000,1,0)
| where is_compliant=0
| table user hire_date latest_ack_version latest_ack_date is_compliant
description: Surfaces active employees who have not acknowledged the current code-of-conduct version within the last 12 months.
value: Removes the audit finding 'CoC acknowledgement trail is incomplete' by making HR's coverage measurable daily.
implementation: (1) Nightly HR roster sync; (2) maintain coc_versions.csv; (3) schedule UC daily; (4) is_compliant=0 triggers an HR reminder email; (5) quarterly compliance report. Indexes required: `index=hr`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=hr:employee`, `sourcetype=lms:acknowledgement`, `sourcetype=lms:acknowledgement;`.
visualization: Table of non-acknowledging users, bar chart by department, single value 'non-compliant users'.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Change data model (Change.All_Changes).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `hr` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `CC1.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that SOC-2 CC1.1 (Integrity and ethical values) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.8.36: SOC 2 CC1.1 — Integrity and ethical values: code-of-conduct acknowledgement trail." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `hr` under `hr:employee`, `lms:acknowledgement`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Nightly HR roster sync. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** maintain coc_versions.csv. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC daily. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** is_compliant=0 triggers an HR reminder email. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** quarterly compliance report. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "SOC 2 CC1.1 — Integrity and ethical values: code-of-conduct acknowledgement trail," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the SOC-2 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search uses a subsearch (`[search ...]`) to dynamically compute a value list that filters the outer search. Subsearches are capped at 50000 results (default `subsearch_maxout`) and 60 seconds wall-clock (`subsearch_maxtime`) — if either limit is hit, the subsearch returns truncated results silently and the outer search under-reports.

Operational alternative: for compliance use cases where completeness matters more than query convenience, consider materialising the subsearch result into a lookup via `| outputlookup` on a scheduled search, then replacing the subsearch with `[| inputlookup ...]`. This decouples the two computations, lifts the truncation risk, and makes the intermediate result auditable.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Change` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Change` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.8.36: SOC 2 CC1.1 — Integrity and ethical values: code-of-conduct ]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.8.36,reg=SOC-2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=hr sourcetype=hr:employee earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `user`, `hire_date`, `module`, `version`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=hr sourcetype=hr:employee earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table user, hire_date, module, version, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.8.36: SOC-2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Non-Acknowledging Users (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart By Department (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Non-Compliant Users' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**SOC-2-specific failure modes:**

• Trust Services Criteria scope: SOC 2 covers five TSCs (Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy). Every UC must specify which TSCs it evidences; 'SOC 2' alone is ambiguous. Most organisations scope to Security + Availability — don't assume all five.

• Type 1 vs Type 2: Type 1 is point-in-time; Type 2 is 6-12 months of evidence. Detections supporting Type 2 must produce date-stamped evidence continuously, not just at snapshots — gaps in evidence are findings.

• CSOC (Control Owner / Service Organisation Control) assertions: SOC 2 depends on management's written assertion about the control. Detection output must be reconcilable to the assertion wording; a mismatch between 'what the control says' and 'what the detection measures' invalidates the evidence.

• Carve-out vs inclusive method: if subservice organisations are carved out, the UC must not cover them (they're not in scope). If inclusive, the UC must cover them even if owned by a third party — clarify in the UC metadata.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for CC1.1 — Integrity and ethical values: code-of-conduct acknowledgement trail. SOC 2 is how we show we run our services responsibly, and we keep evidence that supports that story.
